era_context: The late Archaic period in Greece transitioning toward the Classical period; the reign of Darius I in the Achaemenid Persian Empire; the Spring and Autumn period in China; the Iron Age in the Mediterranean. This year marks a watershed moment in Greek political history with the fall of the Athenian tyranny and the beginning of democratic governance.

events.title: Fall of Hippias and the Peisistratid tyranny in Athens
events.description: Hippias, the tyrant of Athens, was overthrown and expelled from the city. The immediate cause was a conspiracy involving the Alcmaeonid family, who had been exiled by the tyrants and sought to restore their power. However, the broader context included the assassination of Hipparchus in 514 BCE, Cleisthenes' democratic reforms, and growing resentment of tyrannical rule. The Alcmaeonids sought help from Sparta, which sent an army under King Cleomenes I to support the coup. Hippias fled to Persia, where he was granted refuge by Darius I. The fall of the tyranny opened the way for the full implementation of Cleisthenes' democratic reforms and the emergence of Athens as a democratic city-state.
key_figures: Darius I (Achaemenid Great King, granted Hippias refuge)

sources.name: Thucydides, History of the Peloponnesian War, Book VI

events.certainty_note: The fall of Hippias is attested in multiple ancient sources (Aristotle, Thucydides, Herodotus) and is accepted as historical fact by modern scholarship. The date 510 BCE is conventional and widely accepted, though some scholars argue for 511 BCE. The event is one of the best-documented of the Archaic period.
cross_references: Leads to development of Athenian democracy

events.title: Cleisthenes consolidates democratic reforms in Athens
events.description: Following the fall of Hippias, Cleisthenes' democratic reforms were fully implemented and consolidated. The new tribal system (phylai), the Council of 500 (Boule), and the institution of ostracism became the foundation of Athenian democratic governance. These reforms redistributed political power away from aristocratic clans and toward the broader citizen body (though still excluding women, slaves, and non-landowners). The reforms represented a revolutionary shift in political organization and laid the groundwork for the development of Athenian democracy over the following decades.
events.certainty_note: The implementation of Cleisthenes' reforms is well-attested in ancient sources. The date 510 BCE is conventional for the consolidation of these reforms following the fall of Hippias, though the process was gradual and extended over several years.
cross_references: Follows from -513-002 and -512-002 (earlier phases of reforms)
Leads to development of Athenian democracy

events.title: Darius I grants refuge to Hippias
events.description: Hippias, the deposed tyrant of Athens, fled to the Achaemenid Persian Empire and was granted refuge by Darius I. This act had significant long-term consequences: Hippias became an advisor to the Persian court and later encouraged Darius I and his successor Xerxes I to invade Greece, arguing that he could be restored to power with Persian military support. The presence of Hippias at the Persian court thus became a factor in the eventual outbreak of the Greco-Persian Wars.

sources.name: Herodotus, Histories, Book V and VI
Thucydides, History of the Peloponnesian War, Book VI

events.certainty_note: The fact that Hippias was granted refuge by Darius I is attested in ancient sources, but the precise date and circumstances are uncertain. The date 510 BCE is assigned based on the fall of Hippias, but the actual granting of refuge may have occurred slightly later.
cross_references: Leads to -490-001 (Battle of Marathon, where Hippias appears with the Persian army)

events.title: Spartan intervention in Athens under Cleomenes I
events.description: King Cleomenes I of Sparta led a military expedition to Athens to support the Alcmaeonid faction in their coup against Hippias. The Spartan intervention was decisive in securing the fall of the tyranny. However, Cleomenes' motives were not purely altruistic; Sparta sought to establish influence over Athens and prevent the rise of a rival power. The intervention marked the beginning of Spartan involvement in Athenian affairs, which would continue to shape Greek politics for decades.
events.certainty_note: Spartan intervention in Athens is well-attested in ancient sources. The date 510 BCE is conventional and widely accepted.

disconfirming_evidence: The narrative of the fall of Hippias is complicated by several factors: (1) Some scholars argue that Cleisthenes' reforms were not as 'democratic' as later sources suggest; they primarily redistributed power among the aristocracy. (2) The role of Spartan intervention is debated; some sources suggest Cleomenes I later regretted supporting the Alcmaeonids and attempted to restore Hippias. (3) The extent to which the fall of Hippias was a 'popular' uprising versus an aristocratic coup is disputed. (4) The date 510 BCE is conventional; some scholars argue for 511 BCE or even 509 BCE.
historiographic_note: The fall of Hippias is one of the best-documented events of the Archaic period, attested in multiple ancient sources (Aristotle, Thucydides, Herodotus). However, all these sources were written well after the events (Herodotus ~80 years later, Aristotle ~200 years later) and reflect the ideological perspectives of later periods. The sources emphasize the 'democratic' character of the reforms, but modern scholarship recognizes that the reforms primarily benefited the aristocracy and excluded the majority of the population. The historical record is heavily biased toward elite political events in the Mediterranean and Near East.

graph_edges.note: The assassination of Hipparchus destabilized the tyranny and contributed to Hippias's overthrow
The fall of Hippias enabled the full implementation of Cleisthenes' democratic reforms
Hippias's expulsion led to his refuge in Persia
Hippias's presence at the Persian court encouraged Persian invasion of Greece
Spartan military intervention was crucial to the fall of Hippias
